title: Polynomial Identity Testing of Read-Once Oblivious Algebraic Branching Progress
speaker: Michael Forbes
abstract: Polynomial Identity Testing (PIT) is the problem of identifying whether a given algebraic circuit computes the identically zero polynomial. It is well-known that this problem can be solved with small error probability by testing whether the circuit evaluates to zero on random evaluation points.

proof_stages.label: The PIT target and ROABP setting
proof_stages.summary: The talk states the quasi-polynomial explicit hitting-set theorem for known-order read-once oblivious algebraic branching programs and situates it within black-box PIT, lower bounds, white-box PIT, and related restricted algebraic models.

proof_stages.label: Partial-derivative rank blueprint
proof_stages.summary: Nisan's partial-derivative/rank measure and the Forbes-Shpilka white-box basis-reduction algorithm are recast as the rank-based method that the black-box construction will have to simulate.

proof_stages.label: Parallel halving via local mergers
proof_stages.summary: The theorem is reformulated as a small-seed polynomial generator, and the construction is reduced to merging adjacent variable pairs with few bad seed values so that all pairs can be merged in parallel for logarithmically many rounds.

proof_stages.label: Matrix-span invariant
proof_stages.summary: After explaining why Kronecker substitution and mere nonzeroness preservation are inadequate, the proof introduces the matrix span of coefficient/evaluation matrices and proves the bilinear span-preservation lemma that lets left and right blocks be recursively merged independently.

proof_stages.label: Span-preserving curves suffice
proof_stages.summary: The desired local merger is formalized as seed-dependent curves (p_alpha(z),q_alpha(z)) that preserve the matrix span of A(x)B(y) for all but polynomially many seeds, and such curves are shown to imply a recursive generator, up to a degree-growth problem.

proof_stages.label: Vandermonde rank extraction builds the curves
proof_stages.summary: A Vandermonde-style seeded rank extractor compresses the coefficient span of a matrix polynomial to r^2 evaluations, and interpolating through the resulting r^4 product evaluation points produces the span-preserving curves.

proof_stages.label: Low-degree recomposition
proof_stages.summary: To avoid quasi-polynomial degree from naive composition, the proof opens the curves at each level, re-evaluates them at extractor points, and interpolates fresh degree-r^4 curves, preserving span while keeping the generator degree small.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and limitations
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion records black-box reductions for diagonal circuits and noncommutative ABPs, frames the result as a black-box analogue of the partial-derivative method and Boolean small-space generators, and lists fixed-order and seed-length open problems.

examples.note: The speaker exhibits polynomial matrices A(x) and B(y) for which separately preserving nonzeroness can fail after an inappropriate merge, visibly forcing the stronger matrix-span invariant.
